paper_title: ArchKalmag14k: A Kalman‐Filter Based Global Geomagnetic Model for the Holocene

methods.description: Describes the mathematical formulation used to relate geomagnetic observations (declination D, inclination I, and intensity F) to model predictions within a Gaussian Process (GP) framework, including handling observation errors and dating uncertainties.

steps.description: To apply GP based modeling, one has to construct a normal likelihood, relating observations to model predictions of the magnetic field.
steps.searchable_summary: construct normal likelihood function for geomagnetic observations
inputs: observed geomagnetic field data (D, I, F)

steps.description: summarize the linearized expressions as H lin.
steps.searchable_summary: linearize the relationship between observations and model predictions

steps.description: Approximate this two dimensional distribution [Fisher-von Mises] with 95% confidence cone (α 95 ) by two centered normal distributions with standard deviations (Piper, 1989; where o I is the observed inclination. We label these approximate errors E prox. .
steps.searchable_summary: approximate non-Gaussian observation errors with centered normal distributions

steps.description: consider dating uncertainties as suggested in SMKH21. The precise times t at which the archeomagnetic specimen received their magnetization are unknown. Instead, a corrupted date t o = t + e t is reported, and we consider e t to be a centered normal error... This error in the inputs is handled by another linearization, as proposed by McHutchon and Rasmussen (2011, the noisy input Gaussian process (NIGP)).
steps.searchable_summary: handle dating uncertainties using the noisy input Gaussian process (NIGP) method

steps.description: via linearization the dating uncertainties are translated into observation uncertainties, and the covariance gets an additional term Σ tt′ is the covariance matrix of the dating errors and • is the Hadamard product... The effect of the NIGP model is thus the inclusion of dating errors as contributions to the data covariance, similar to measurement errors. To realize this, dating errors are weighed by the second order time derivative of the kernel.
steps.searchable_summary: translate dating uncertainties into observation covariance contribution using NIGP
inputs: second order time derivative of the kernel
outputs: additional term for data covariance matrix

steps.description: Finally, a residual term ξP, where ∼  (0, ) is added to cover any effects that are not modeled, like crustal field or ellipticity of the Earth
steps.searchable_summary: add a residual term to the data model for unmodeled effects
outputs: final data model equation with residual

methods.method_name: Geomagnetic Data Set Curation and Outlier Detection
methods.description: Describes the process of compiling, curating, and filtering the archeomagnetic and volcanic data set used for the model, including correcting known errors and identifying outliers using a Naive Bayes classifier integrated into the Kalman filter.

steps.description: The data set is a slight variation of all records from the archeological and volcanic database from GEOMAGIA v3.4 (Brown et al., 2015) with ages between 12000 BCE and 2000 CE.
steps.searchable_summary: compile geomagnetic records from GEOMAGIA database within specified age range

steps.description: Part of the records from Mexico contain wrong age and dating uncertainty estimates... Some of them are too old by several thousand years and have been removed, while for others updated 14 C ages have been published... These updates have been used (see Table S1 in Supporting Information S1)
steps.searchable_summary: identify and correct or remove records with known age and dating uncertainty errors
inputs: list of known errors/updates (e.g., Table S1)

steps.description: To identify other records that deviate from the rest, we use a Naive Bayes classifier (e.g., Berrar, 2018). This procedure is integrated into the Kalman-filter as follows:
steps.searchable_summary: integrate a Naive Bayes classifier into the Kalman filter for outlier detection
outputs: Kalman filter with integrated outlier detection module

steps.description: When a step i + 1 contains new data, we evaluate the probability of every record to either come from a normal distribution with standard deviation of the size of the reported error or from a flat distribution of larger variance ((100°) 2 for declination, (50°) 2 for inclination and (100 µT) 2 for intensities).
steps.searchable_summary: evaluate probability of a data record belonging to a normal vs flat distribution
inputs: parameters for normal distribution (based on reported error)

steps.description: Records that are more likely to stem from the flat distribution are considered outliers.
steps.searchable_summary: identify records as outliers if more likely from flat distribution

steps.description: By this procedure 276 records are identified and removed from the data set.
steps.searchable_summary: remove identified outlier records from the data set

steps.description: The final data set contains 18,735 records from 11,637 locations. It consists of 5,611 declinations, 7,028 inclinations, and 6,096 intensities.
steps.searchable_summary: report the size and composition of the final filtered data set
outputs: summary statistics (total records, locations, D/I/F counts)
